questions.passage:
Where does a mind stop and the rest of the world begin? Common sense answers with anatomy: thinking happens inside the head, and everything past the skin is mere environment. A well-known philosophical thought experiment invites us to doubt this. Imagine Otto, whose memory is failing and who therefore carries a notebook everywhere. When he wants to visit the museum, he consults the notebook, finds the address he long ago recorded there, and goes. Inga, by contrast, simply remembers the address. The proponents of the extended mind ask what principled difference separates the two cases. Otto's notebook is always available, he consults it automatically, he trusts what he finds there without argument, and its entries were endorsed by him when written. If information playing exactly that role inside Inga's skull counts as belief, why should the same functional role, implemented in ink, count as something less?

The underlying principle is one of parity: if a process in the world functions in the way a cognitive process functions in the head, we should not deny it cognitive status merely because of its location. On this view, the mind is not a fixed organ but a shifting coalition of brain, body, and instruments. The sailor with her charts, the accountant with his ledger, and anyone whose plans live in a calendar are not merely aided by these things; they think with them, and partly in them.

Critics reply that the argument trades on a confusion. From the fact that a notebook is tightly coupled to Otto's thinking, it does not follow that the notebook is part of that thinking; the wind is coupled to the sailboat without being part of the boat. Cognition, these critics argue, is marked by features that notebooks lack. Biological memory is integrated with everything else a person knows: it updates silently, colors perception, and is revised without deliberate effort when new information arrives. A notebook entry sits inert until consulted, cannot correct itself, and is trusted only because its owner decides to trust it. To call both belief, the critics say, is to stretch a useful word until it no longer marks a difference worth marking.

Defenders have answers, and the exchange has continued for decades with the peculiar stamina of disputes in which each side suspects the other of merely redefining terms. What strikes an observer of the debate, however, is how much both sides concede. The critics grant that tools can transform what a thinker accomplishes; the defenders grant that notebooks and brains differ in texture, speed, and integration. The disagreement narrows to whether these admitted differences matter enough to fix a boundary, and boundaries of that sort are settled by the purposes of our concepts as much as by the facts.

Perhaps, then, the thesis is best judged not as a discovery about where minds truly end but as a proposal about how the concept of mind might earn its keep. Taken as metaphysics, the claim that Otto's notebook literally contains his beliefs invites endless and possibly empty dispute. Taken as a corrective, it does honest work: it reminds us that the traditional boundary at the skull was never argued for so much as assumed, and that a science of cognition which studies only what happens inside the head will misdescribe thinkers whose achievements are inseparable from their instruments. One need not decide that the notebook is mind in order to concede that the old border was drawn by habit rather than by principle. That concession is smaller than the thesis, and it may be the durable part.
questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the passage's central claim?

choices.A: The extended mind thesis has been decisively and permanently refuted by the simple observation that coupling a tool to a thinker does not make the tool a genuine part of thought.
choices.B: Cognitive science should abandon the study of internal processes in favor of studying thinkers together with their instruments.
choices.C: Whatever the fate of its bold metaphysical claim, the extended mind thesis succeeds as a corrective to an assumed rather than argued boundary at the skull.
choices.D: The dispute over the extended mind will be resolved once psychologists identify the biological features that all cognitive processes share.

explanations.A: The coupling objection is presented as the critics' reply, not as the passage's verdict; the author goes on to note how much the critics themselves concede.
explanations.B: The passage warns that studying only what happens inside the head will misdescribe some thinkers, which calls for widening attention, not abandoning internal processes.
explanations.C: Correct. The final paragraph distinguishes the thesis taken as metaphysics, which invites 'endless and possibly empty dispute,' from the thesis taken as a corrective, which does 'honest work' by exposing a boundary drawn by habit; that smaller concession is called the durable part.
explanations.D: The author suggests the boundary dispute turns on the purposes of our concepts as much as on facts, so a purely biological discovery is exactly what he doubts could settle it.

questions.stem: The passage introduces the case of Otto and his notebook primarily in order to:

choices.A: give a concrete illustration of the parity principle on which the extended mind thesis rests.
choices.B: show that failing biological memory can be fully and reliably compensated for by carefully maintained external records.
choices.C: demonstrate that written records are more reliable than the recollections they replace.
choices.D: establish that Otto and Inga hold identical beliefs about the museum.

explanations.A: Correct. The example sets up the question of what principled difference separates Otto from Inga, and the next paragraph names the underlying idea as one of parity: equivalent functional role should mean equivalent cognitive status regardless of location.
explanations.B: The passage uses the notebook to raise a conceptual question about the boundary of mind, not to assess how completely external aids compensate for memory loss.
explanations.C: No comparison of reliability is drawn; indeed the critics later stress that notebook entries cannot correct themselves, a point against their superiority.
explanations.D: Whether the two cases deserve the same description is precisely what the example puts in dispute; the passage does not assert their identity but asks what could justify denying it.

questions.stem: The author would most likely DISAGREE with which of the following statements?

choices.A: The differences between notebooks and biological memory in speed and integration are real.
choices.B: Some long-running philosophical disputes persist partly because each side suspects the other of redefining terms.
choices.C: A science of cognition can mislead if it treats a thinker's instruments as irrelevant to what the thinker accomplishes.
choices.D: The traditional placement of the mind's boundary at the skull rests on a carefully constructed argument.

explanations.A: The author explicitly notes that defenders of the thesis grant these differences in texture, speed, and integration, and he endorses that concession as part of what narrows the debate.
explanations.B: The author himself remarks that the exchange has the stamina 'of disputes in which each side suspects the other of merely redefining terms,' so he affirms this.
explanations.C: The passage warns that studying only what happens inside the head 'will misdescribe thinkers whose achievements are inseparable from their instruments,' which is this very point.
explanations.D: Correct. The author's closing judgment is that the boundary at the skull 'was never argued for so much as assumed' and was 'drawn by habit rather than by principle,' the direct denial of the claim that it rests on careful argument.

questions.stem: Which of the following cases best fits the parity principle as the passage describes it?

choices.A: A translator who consults a worn phrasebook automatically, trusts its entries without deliberation, and carries it wherever she works.
choices.B: A student who looks up a single unfamiliar term in a library reference dictionary one afternoon and never afterward returns to consult it.
choices.C: A commuter who distrusts his printed timetable and telephones the station to verify every departure.
choices.D: A researcher who stores raw data on a server she cannot access while traveling.

explanations.A: Correct. The phrasebook mirrors the notebook's profile as the passage draws it: constant availability, automatic consultation, and unargued trust, the very features said to give Otto's entries the functional role of belief.
explanations.B: A single consultation lacks the standing availability and habitual reliance that the parity argument requires; the dictionary functions as an occasional resource, not as memory.
explanations.C: Verifying every departure is the opposite of trusting what one finds without argument, so the timetable fails one of the conditions the passage lists.
explanations.D: Information unavailable during travel fails the availability condition outright; the parity case depends on the resource being reliably at hand.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings, if established, would most weaken the parity argument as the passage presents it?

choices.A: Most people who keep detailed calendars and planners report feeling profoundly dependent on them for organizing their daily lives, appointments, and long-term commitments.
choices.B: Sailors using charts reach their destinations more often than sailors navigating from memory.
choices.C: People like Otto in fact subject their own notebook entries to deliberate scrutiny before acting on them, unlike their spontaneous recollections.
choices.D: The philosophers who proposed the thought experiment later applied it to devices other than notebooks.

explanations.A: Felt dependence on calendars is compatible with, and even suggestive of, the deep coupling the thesis describes; it does nothing to break the claimed functional equivalence.
explanations.B: Superior outcomes with charts would show tools help, a point the critics already grant, without touching the question of whether the chart's role matches biological memory's.
explanations.C: Correct. The parity argument turns on Otto trusting his entries automatically and without argument, just as Inga trusts recollection; if notebook users in fact interpose deliberate scrutiny, the functional roles diverge at precisely the point the analogy needs them to match.
explanations.D: How widely the proposers later applied their example bears on the thesis's ambitions, not on whether the original functional parallel holds.

questions.stem: The tone of the passage is best described as:

choices.A: polemical, pressing a single side of the dispute to victory.
choices.B: even-handed and appraising, weighing both positions before offering a qualified verdict.
choices.C: nostalgic, lamenting the loss of an older and simpler picture of the mind.
choices.D: ironic, mocking professional philosophers for spending decades debating a question that admits no possible answer.

explanations.A: The passage gives the critics their strongest objection in full and faults the thesis 'taken as metaphysics'; no side is pressed to victory.
explanations.B: Correct. The author states each position sympathetically, observes how much both sides concede, and closes with a deliberately limited verdict that the thesis's corrective function 'may be the durable part,' the signature of appraisal rather than advocacy.
explanations.C: The common-sense picture of the mind bounded by the skull is treated as an unargued habit, not as a lost ideal to be mourned.
explanations.D: Although the author notes the debate's stamina with some wryness, he insists the corrective reading does 'honest work,' which is the opposite of dismissing the question as unanswerable.
